draft.introduction: At Meno 80d–e, Meno poses the paradox of inquiry: how can you search for something you do not know? Socrates responds with the doctrine of recollection—the soul, being immortal, has learned all things and needs only to be reminded.

translation.text: How will you search for something, Socrates, when you do not know at all what it is? Which of the things you do not know will you set up as the object of your search? And even if you should stumble upon it, how will you know that this is the thing you did not know? The soul, being immortal and having been born many times, has learned all things.
translation.original_text: καὶ τίνα τρόπον ζητήσεις, ὦ Σώκρατες, τοῦτο ὃ μὴ οἶσθα τὸ παράπαν ὅτι ἐστιν;

translator_notes.note: The doctrine of anamnesis (recollection) implies that all learning is recovery of knowledge the soul already possesses from its pre-embodied existence. This became foundational for Neoplatonic epistemology and the idea that the soul contains within itself the pattern of all reality.
